script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Monday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed one point nine nine percent down on the session. It was a broad and painful selloff concentrated in semiconductors, energy-sensitive names, and anything tied to the memory supply chain.

President Trump moved Monday to reinstate a United States naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. Oil jumped above seventy-nine dollars a barrel. The Nasdaq sold off hard. Memory chipmakers led the decline, and the damage across this portfolio was immediate and significant.

The Nasdaq composite closed lower by a meaningful margin Monday, pressured by a combination of spiking crude oil prices and renewed geopolitical risk stemming from the Strait of Hormuz announcement. There was no fresh Federal Reserve commentary or economic data to offset the macro headwind. The move in oil functioned as a de facto tightening signal for growth and technology equities, and the index performance reflected exactly that.

The top story Monday belongs to the memory sector, and no name felt it more directly than Micron Technology. Micron closed down four point three two percent, but the headline from finance dot yahoo dot com tells a more complex story. The company announced a three billion dollar equity investment in GlobalWafers, the Taiwanese silicon wafer producer, as part of a broader domestic semiconductor expansion plan that now exceeds two hundred fifty billion dollars. The stated rationale is raw material security — locking in silicon wafer supply ahead of what Micron's leadership sees as a sustained surge in artificial intelligence memory demand. Strategically, this is a long-term defensive move. But markets are not rewarding long-term thinking on a day when oil is spiking and memory ETFs are slipping into bear market territory. Sandisk and SK Hynix dived alongside Micron. The GlobalWafers investment signals that Micron sees structural supply constraints ahead. That matters for this portfolio, where Micron represents five point seven five percent of total weight. The near-term pressure is real. So is the strategic rationale.

NVIDIA closed at two hundred three dollars and fifty-three cents, down three point five two percent on the session. Monday's macro environment was the primary driver — renewed Strait of Hormuz tensions rattled growth-sensitive names broadly, and NVIDIA, as the highest-weighted position at seven point six percent of the portfolio, absorbed meaningful damage in absolute terms. Cboe Global Markets announced plans to launch extended trading hours for single-stock options, and NVIDIA is one of the names cited as a primary beneficiary given its elevated implied volatility profile. That development has no immediate price catalyst but reinforces the degree to which NVIDIA remains one of the most actively traded derivatives instruments in the market. Watch for any institutional commentary around artificial intelligence spending as earnings season approaches.

Apple closed at three hundred seventeen dollars and thirty-one cents, up zero point six three percent on the session — one of the few primary holdings to close in the green. Counterpoint Research data released Monday showed that while global smartphone shipments fell to a thirteen-year low in the second quarter, Apple's iPhone sales held up alongside Google's Pixel line. There is also a developing narrative around artificial intelligence partnerships: reports suggest Apple and Alphabet may deepen their collaboration following legal friction between Apple and OpenAI. That dynamic is worth monitoring, as it has implications for both names in this portfolio.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-two dollars and fifty-one cents, down one point three one percent. The Apple and Alphabet artificial intelligence alignment story was the notable development, with analyst Paul Meeks of Freedom Capital Markets quoted as saying, quote, the bad blood between Apple and OpenAI plays into Google's hands, end quote. Alphabet's relative underperformance versus Apple on the same macro backdrop likely reflects broader communication services pressure and the continuing data center cost story circulating around the sector.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred thirty-seven dollars, down four point three two percent, as covered in the top story. The GlobalWafers investment and the sector-wide memory selloff dominated the narrative.

Microsoft closed at three hundred ninety dollars and ninety-nine cents, up one point five three percent — a clear outperformer in Monday's session. With four point five two percent of portfolio weight, the gain was a meaningful offset to the broader damage. There was no major company-specific catalyst. Microsoft's relative strength in a down tape speaks to its defensive positioning within the technology sector.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-seven dollars and thirty-one cents, up zero point eight zero percent. No major company-specific news drove the move. Amazon's consumer discretionary classification made its green close notable given broader market weakness. The name held its footing well.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred thirty-four dollars and thirty-nine cents, down four point two one percent. No company-specific news was the driver here — the decline mirrors the broader semiconductor sector pressure triggered by the Strait of Hormuz announcement and the memory chain selloff. Advanced Micro Devices' artificial intelligence chip exposure makes it highly correlated to NVIDIA on macro risk days like Monday.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and seventy-six cents, down three point one nine percent. No company-specific headlines drove the decline. The name traded in line with broader risk-off sentiment, consistent with its beta profile. At three point zero nine percent of the portfolio, the damage was contained but notable.

Intel closed at one hundred three dollars and twelve cents, down six point one two percent — the worst single-session percentage decline among primary holdings. At two point nine zero percent of the portfolio, Intel's underperformance was disproportionate. There was no company-specific news to explain the divergence. Intel's position at the lower end of both portfolio weight and competitive standing in artificial intelligence chip development makes it a focal point for sector rotation pressure on risk-off sessions. Watch for any analyst commentary or earnings preview notes heading into reporting season.

Turning now to three data points that matter in the next forty-eight hours. First, oil price momentum following Monday's Strait of Hormuz announcement — any escalation or diplomatic response from Iran will directly influence how markets price energy cost risk for large-scale data center operators, including names like Microsoft and Amazon. Second, watch for any Federal Reserve speaker commentary scheduled for Tuesday — rate expectations remain a primary lever for technology equity valuations, and any shift in tone after Monday's macro disruption will matter. Third, earnings season is approaching, and Micron Technology's next scheduled report will be a direct read on artificial intelligence memory demand and whether the GlobalWafers investment thesis holds against near-term demand signals. Timing and guidance language will be critical for a name at five point seven five percent of this portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the Strait of Hormuz situation closely — if oil holds above seventy-nine dollars, the pressure on semiconductor and growth names is not finished.
